item.title: Twitter预计将裁员约一半 | Twitter is expected to lay off about half of its workforce
item.description:
本·哈尔彭

发表于根据The Verge 的这个故事

马斯克预计将裁减 Twitter 约 7,500 名员工中的大约一半。 据参与讨论的员工称，他的外部顾问团队上周一直在根据他们最近对 Twitter 代码库的贡献来决定保留哪些工程师和技术经理。

您对这种情况的最新情况有何看法？ 您对一般事物的感觉如何。 很想听听你的意见。

约翰内斯·凯特曼 - 一旦暂停，本将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，本将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，本的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 ben 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 ben 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Ben Halpern

Posted on Nov 4

Per this story by The Verge

Musk is expected to cut roughly half of Twitter’s roughly 7,500-person workforce. His team of outside advisors has spent the last week determining which engineers and technical managers to keep based largely on their recent contributions to Twitter’s codebase, according to employees involved in the discussions.

Stripe also underwent significant layoffs yesterday, as have others in the tech industry, in a "second wave" of sorts.

What are your thoughts about the latest in this situation? How are you feeling about things in general. Would love to hear from you.

Johannes Kettmann - Sep 30

item.description:

item.title: 这是 2022 年的啤酒节 | This was Hacktoberfest 2022
item.description:
在这一年的过程中，我尝试了几次进入开源领域。 我感到困惑、不知所措，最后只发了一两篇关于旨在引导初学者进入开源的非常简单的主题的帖子。

目录

我终于为开源做出了贡献

我想，也许开源不适合我，我不够聪明，无法为开源做出贡献。
但是由于 Hacktoberfest 即将到来，每个人都对此感到兴奋，我真的很想成为其中的一员😇。

我可以自豪地说，我今年成功参加了Hacktoberfest 2022 。

不仅作为贡献者，而且作为维护者。 知道进入开源有多么困难，我想为其他人提供一种进入开源的简单方法，以获取经验并在他们的简历上写点东西。

我必须说，作为维护者的工作对我来说似乎比作为贡献者的工作要容易得多，尽管这并不容易。

我对我的组织 YurisCodingClub 有很多想法，它应该帮助像我这样的职业改变者进入技术领域并分享我的技巧和经验，就像我一年前所做的那样。

当第一个贡献者问我他是否可以解决我的一个问题时，我非常高兴。 由于我已经熟悉 GitHub、GitHub 项目、创建问题和 PR，因此设置项目很快。

我确保这些问题对初学者友好，并且还有纯 HTML 或 CSS任务，以便每个人都可以做出贡献。 我还包括了UX 设计任务，并希望增加任务的多样性，以便每个人，无论专注于什么，都可以学习和贡献。

但是当第一个 PR 进来时，我有点不知所措。 我知道（自己贡献了）贡献者希望快速处理 PR，以便获得hacktoberfest 接受的徽章。

这让我很紧张，所以我没有看代码就直接接受了前几个 PR 🙈。

但经过几天和一些规划，我更彻底地查看了 PR，给出了如何改进代码的提示和建议，与贡献者的沟通非常棒🥳。

如果您有兴趣贡献，请查看我在 GitHub 上的组织 YurisCodingClub 。

我很自豪能够参与所有关于可访问性、社区和社会公益的项目。 对我来说最重要的话题😊。

我的票相对简单，主要是小的 CSS 定制或将附加内容写入 JSON 文件。

最有趣的是查看其他项目的测试，它们的不同设置和文件夹结构，以及 esLint 规则——其中一些我现在用于我自己的项目。 通过查看和阅读这些项目的代码和设置，我学到了很多东西。

但最好的时刻是当我向FreeCodeCamp提出建议，包括专门针对Developer Quiz的可访问性问题。 这必须先由昆西审查。

几天后，我得到了他（！！）同意我的建议的信息，现在您还可以了解可访问性。
本月最有意义的时刻🥰。

他们在这里，我的成就徽章😄

我没有为任何有兴趣的人都可以参与的项目做出贡献，并且他们接受了第一个好的 PR 🙅🏽‍♀️。

我发现这种方法相当令人沮丧。 我更喜欢并发现被正式分配更有用，然后有足够的时间将 100% 的注意力集中在票上。

你怎么看待这件事？

感谢您的阅读和时间。 对此，我真的非常感激！



原文:

Over the course of this year, I've tried a few times to get into open source. I was confused, overwhelmed, and ended up making only one or two posts on really simple topics designed to guide beginners into open source.

Table of contents

I Finally Contributed To Open Source

I thought, maybe open source isn't for me, I'm not smart enough to contribute to open source.
But since Hacktoberfest was coming up and everyone was excited about it, I really wanted to be a part of it 😇.

I can proudly say that I successfully participated in Hacktoberfest 2022 this year.

And not only as a contributor, but also as a maintainer. Knowing how hard it is to get into open source, I want to provide an easy way for others to get into open source as well to gain experience and have something to write on their CV.

Hacktoberfest As A Maintainer

I must say that the work as a maintainer seemed much easier to me than the work as a contributor, although it was not easy at all.

I have a lot of ideas for my organization YurisCodingClub, which is supposed to help career changers like me to get into tech and share my tips and experience, like I did a year ago.

I was very happy when the first contributor asked me if he could work on one of my issues. Since I'm already familiar with GitHub, GitHub projects, creating Issues and PRs, setting up the project was quick.

I made sure that the issues are beginner-friendly and that there are also pure HTML or CSS tasks so that everyone can contribute. I've also included UX design tasks and want to increase the variety of tasks so that everyone, regardless of focus, can learn and contribute.

But when the first PR came in, I was a little overwhelmed. I knew (having contributed myself) that the contributor wanted the PR to be handled quickly so it could get the hacktoberfest-accepted badge.

This made me pretty nervous, so I accepted the first few PRs right away without much looking at the code 🙈.

But after a few days and some planning, I looked at the PRs more thoroughly, gave tips and suggestions on how to improve the code, and communication with the contributors was great 🥳.

If you are interested in contributing check out my organization YurisCodingClub on GitHub.

Hacktoberfest As A Contributor

I am very proud to have been involved in projects that are all about accessibility, community and social good. The most important topics for me 😊.

My tickets were relatively simple, mostly small CSS customization or writing additional content into a JSON file.

The most interesting thing was to see other projects' tests, their different setups and folder structures, and esLint rules - some of which I now use for my own projects. I learned a lot from looking at and reading through the code and setup of these projects.

But the best moment was when I made a suggestion to FreeCodeCamp to include accessibility questions specifically for the Developer Quiz. This had to be reviewed by Quincy first.

After a few days I got the info that he (!!) agrees with my suggestion and now you can also learn about accessibility.
Most rewarding moment of this month 🥰.

And here they are, my achievement badges 😄

I have not contributed to projects that said anyone interested can work on it and they accept the first good PR that comes in 🙅🏽‍♀️.

I find this approach rather demotivating. I prefer and find it more useful to be officially assigned and then get enough time to focus 100% on the ticket.

What do you think about this?

Thanks for your reading and time. I really appreciate it!
item.author: yuridevat

item.title: 用 Rust 解析 JSON | Parsing JSON with Rust
item.description:
在我学习 Rust 的过程中，我一直在寻找简单实用的项目来了解基本概念并增加我的语法肌肉记忆。 我发现自己在大多数语言中每天都在做的一件事是从不同的来源加载数据，比如文本文件，或者更结构化的来源，比如 JSON 或 YAML。

所以我开始思考，在 Rust 中解析 JSON 会是什么样子？ 不过不是从头开始——我们对在这里构建解析器不感兴趣。 理想情况下，我们会使用内部 Rust 方法或 3rd 方 crate。

在本文中，我将介绍如何使用serde和serde-json读取、解析 JSON 并将其序列化为 Rust 结构，以便在 Rust 应用程序中使用。 你可以在 Github 上找到最终的源代码。

建立一个新项目

让我们创建一个新的 Rust 应用程序来编写代码。 您也可以克隆此分支并跳过此步骤。

进入内部后，安装serde和serde_json 。 我们还将为 serde 添加derive功能，稍后我们将使用该功能。

 cargo add serde --features derive cargo add serde_json

现在让我们制作我们的解析器！ 在src/parser.rs创建一个新文件，它将包含我们所有的解析逻辑。

如果您查看serde_json README 文件，您可以找到一些关于如何使用开箱即用的库的精彩示例。 让我们尝试其中一个，看看我们是否正确安装了所有东西：

 use serde_json ::{ Result , Value }; pub fn untyped_example () -> Result < () > { // Some JSON input data as a &str. Maybe this comes from the user. let data = r#" { "name": "John Doe", "age": 43, "phones": [ "+44 1234567", "+44 2345678" ] }"# ; // Parse the string of data into serde_json::Value. let v : Value = serde_json :: from_str ( data ) ? ; // Access parts of the data by indexing with square brackets. println! ( "Please call {} at the number {}" , v [ "name" ], v [ "phones" ][ 0 ]); Ok (()) }

现在我们可以在src/main.rs中使用这个函数

// "Import" anything public in the parser module pub mod parser ; fn main () { println! ( "Hello, world!" ); // Parse the JSON let result = parser :: untyped_example (); // Handle errors from the parser if any match result { Ok ( result ) => (), Err ( error ) => print! ( "{}" , error ), } }

尝试使用cargo run它。 您应该会在控制台中看到文本“请致电 John Doe...”。 如果没有，我们还添加了一个错误处理match ，它应该从 serde 返回任何错误。

您可以从这个示例中看到库 API 相当容易使用。 它们公开了一个从字符串解析 JSON 的from_str()方法，您可以提供内联（就像我们上面所做的那样） - 或者从本地或远程文件加载（我们稍后会这样做）。 解析 JSON 后，您可以通过 JSON 中的属性或键访问数据（例如v["name"]从{ "name": "John Doe" }中获取）。

既然我们已经将它集成到我们的应用程序中，让我们看看我们还能用这个库做什么。

从本地文件加载 JSON

为了加载 JSON，我们需要一个 JSON 文件。 所以让我们创建一个。 在项目的根目录创建一个名为data的文件夹。 在其中创建一个test.json文件。

 { "name" : "John Doe" , "age" : 43 , "phones" : [ "+44 1234567" , "+44 2345678" ]
}

要加载数据，我们可以使用Rust 标准库中的 FileSystem API。 我们将在main.rs文件中执行此操作，并将数据（也就是一长串 JSON）传递给解析器。

 // src/main.rs use std :: fs ; pub mod parser ; fn main () { println! ( "Hello, world!" ); // Grab JSON file let file_path = "data/test.json" .to_owned (); let contents = fs :: read_to_string ( file_path ) .expect ( "Couldn't find or load that file." ); parser :: untyped_example ( & contents ); }

我们现在需要修改解析器以接受数据。 您也可以使用内联 JSON 删除data变量，因为我们不再需要它：

 // src/parser.rs pub fn untyped_example ( json_data : & str ) -> Result < () > { let v : Value = serde_json :: from_str ( json_data ) ? ;

尝试使用cargo run它，您应该会得到与以前相同的结果（“请致电...”消息）。

输入数据

但是，如果我们在解析数据之前就知道数据的结构会怎样呢？ 例如，我们可能希望使用System UI / Styled System 规范解析主题，例如Chakra UI 。

这将让我们使用严格类型的struct s 来访问我们的数据——所以我们可以在我们输入v.时在我们的 IDE 中获得自动完成功能，而不是使用v["name"]来访问名称，并且它会完成到v.name 。 这是一种更好的开发人员体验，并为使用不存在的属性创建了更多的安全网。

我们可以将其完全复制粘贴到[parser.rs](http://parser.rs)文件中。

 use serde ::{ Deserialize , Serialize }; use serde_json :: Result ; #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] struct Person { name : String , age : u8 , phones : Vec < String > , } pub fn typed_example () -> Result < () > { // Some JSON input data as a &str. Maybe this comes from the user. let data = r#" { "name": "John Doe", "age": 43, "phones": [ "+44 1234567", "+44 2345678" ] }"# ; // Parse the string of data into a Person object. This is exactly the // same function as the one that produced serde_json::Value above, but // now we are asking it for a Person as output. let p : Person = serde_json :: from_str ( data ) ? ; // Do things just like with any other Rust data structure. println! ( "Please call {} at the number {}" , p .name , p .phones [ 0 ]); Ok (()) }

然后将untyped_example函数替换为main.rs文件中的typed_example函数。

 pub mod parser ; fn main () { println! ( "Hello, world!" ); // You can keep the file system stuff, I removed it for simplicity // we'll use it later in the tutorial parser :: typed_example (); }

如何处理对象类型？

因此，在查看示例后想到的第一个问题——如何处理具有键和属性的对象？ 看来您可以使用HashMap<>类型并为对象键（通常是String ）和对象值（任何东西 - 有时是String - 可能是数字的i32 ）提供类型。

所以说我有一个看起来像这样的 JSON 主题：

 { "animation" : { "default" : "400ms ease-in" , "fast" : "300ms ease-in" }, "breakpoints" : { "mobile" : "320px" , "tablet" : "768px" , "computer" : "992px" , "desktop" : "1200px" , "widescreen" : "1920px" }, "colors" : { "text" : "#111212" , "background" : "#fff" , "primary" : "#005CDD" , "secondary" : "#6D59F0" , "muted" : "#f6f6f9" , "gray" : "#D3D7DA" , "highlight" : "hsla(205, 100%, 40%, 0.125)" , "white" : "#FFF" , "black" : "#111212" }, "fonts" : { "body" : "Roboto, Helvetiva Neue, Helvetica, Aria, sans-serif" , "heading" : "Archivo, Helvetiva Neue, Helvetica, Aria, sans-serif" , "monospace" : "Menlo, monospace" }, "font_sizes" : [ 12 , 14 , 16 , 20 , 24 , 32 , 48 , 64 , 96 ], "font_weights" : { "body" : 400 , "heading" : 500 , "bold" : 700 }, "line_heights" : { "body" : 1.5 , "heading" : 1.25 }, "space" : [ 0 , 4 , 8 , 16 , 32 , 64 , 128 , 256 , 512 ], "sizes" : { "avatar" : 48 }, "radii" : { "default" : 0 , "circle" : 99999 }, "shadows" : { "card" : { "light" : "15px 15px 35px rgba(0, 127, 255, 0.5)" } }, "gradients" : { "primary" : "linear-gradient()" } }

您可以将该类型构造为如下所示：

 use std :: collections :: HashMap ; use serde ::{ Deserialize , Serialize }; use serde_json :: Result ; #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] struct Theme { colors : HashMap < String , String > , space : Vec < i32 > , font_sizes : Vec < i32 > , fonts : HashMap < String , String > , font_weights : HashMap < String , i32 > , line_heights : HashMap < String , f32 > , breakpoints : HashMap < String , String > , animation : HashMap < String , String > , gradients : HashMap < String , String > , }

你可以看到我使用String表示任何基于字符串的值， i32表示数字，特别是f32表示任何“浮点数”，也就是带小数的数字。

解析 JSON 时，会返回一个HashMap ，因此您可以使用get()方法访问其中的数据 - 或使用for循环遍历所有值：

 // Get a single value println! ( "Black: {}" , p .colors .get ( "black" )); // Loop over all the colors for ( key , color ) in p .colors { // Create the custom property let custom_property = format! ( "--colors-{}" , key ); let css_rule = format! ( "{}: {};" , & custom_property , color ); // @TODO: Export a CSS stylesheet file (or return CSS) println! ( "{}" , css_rule ); stylesheet .push ( css_rule ); // Add the custom property theme_tokens .colors .insert ( key , custom_property ); }

这很好用！ 您可以在这里看到我们能够循环颜色，甚至可以根据来自 HashMap 的键和值（也就是颜色名称和值）生成 CSS 自定义属性。

处理可选/多种类型

但是如果我们有可选类型呢？ 还是同一属性的多种类型（例如“大小”单位，可以是数字10或字符串，如10px ）？ 在 Typescript 中，我们可以创建一个像这样的type Size = string | number 在 Rust 中，这相当于一个Enum 。

经过一番研究后，我发现如果将untagged的宏传递给 serde 支持枚举类型：

 #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Debug)] #[serde(untagged)] enum Colors { Name ( String ), Number ( i32 ), } #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] struct Theme { test : Colors , } // ... after parsing let p : Theme = serde_json :: from_str ( json_data ) ? ; println! ( "{:#?}" , p .test );

将以下属性添加到您的 JSON：

这也很好用！ 您可以轻松创建一些“动态”类型，并且仍然根据它们的类型对它们进行相当严格的处理。

将 eyy 放回 JSON 中

我希望这能让你很好地了解如何使用 serde crate 在 Rust 中解析 JSON，以及如何处理不同的用例和数据类型。 您可以使用 JSON（或其他文件类型 - serde 支持很多类似 YAML、TOML 等）创建许多很酷的应用程序。

与往常一样，您可以在我的 Github 上找到本教程的完整代码。

如果您有任何问题或想分享您一直在做的工作，请随时在 Twitter 上联系我。

干杯，
亮

一旦暂停，whoisryosuke 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，whoisryosuke 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦未发表，whoisryosuke 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 whoisryosuke 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 以下是标记 whoisryosuke 的方法：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 whoisryosuke 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

As I’ve been learning Rust, I’ve been looking for easy and practical projects to get my head around basic concepts and increase my syntax muscle memory. One of the things I find myself doing everyday in most languages is loading data from different sources, like text files, or more structured sources like JSON or YAML.

So it got me thinking, how would parsing JSON look like in Rust? Not from scratch though — we’re not interested in building a parser here. Ideally we’d use either internal Rust methods or a 3rd party crate.

In this article I’ll go over how I used serde and serde-json to read, parse, and serialize JSON into Rust structs to use inside Rust apps. You can find the final source code on Github.

Setting up a new project

Let’s create a new Rust app to code inside. You could also clone this branch and skip this step.

Once you navigate inside, install serde and serde_json. We’ll also add the derive feature for serde, which we’ll use later.

cargo add serde --features derive
cargo add serde_json

Now let’s make our parser! Create a new file at src/parser.rs that’ll hold all our parsing logic.

If you look at the serde_json README file, you can find some fantastic examples on how to use the library out of the box. Let’s try one of those to see if we installed everything correctly:

use serde_json::{Result, Value}; pub fn untyped_example() -> Result<()> { // Some JSON input data as a &str. Maybe this comes from the user. let data = r#" { "name": "John Doe", "age": 43, "phones": [ "+44 1234567", "+44 2345678" ] }"#; // Parse the string of data into serde_json::Value. let v: Value = serde_json::from_str(data)?; // Access parts of the data by indexing with square brackets. println!("Please call {} at the number {}", v["name"], v["phones"][0]); Ok(())
}

Now we can use this function inside of our src/main.rs

// "Import" anything public in the parser module
pub mod parser; fn main() { println!("Hello, world!"); // Parse the JSON let result = parser::untyped_example(); // Handle errors from the parser if any match result { Ok(result) => (), Err(error) => print!("{}", error), }
}

Try running this with cargo run. You should see the text “Please call John Doe at…” in your console. If not, we also added an error handling match that should return any errors from serde.

You can see from this example the library API is fairly easy to use. They expose a from_str() method that parses JSON from a string, which you can provide inline (like we did above) - or load from a local or remote file (we’ll do this later). Once the JSON is parsed, you can access the data through the properties or keys in the JSON (e.g. v["name"] grabs from { "name": "John Doe" }).

Let’s see what else we can do with the library now that we have it integrated into our app.

Loading JSON from local file

In order to load JSON, we need a JSON file. So let’s create one. At the root of the project create a folder called data. Inside it, create a test.json file.

{ "name": "John Doe", "age": 43, "phones": [ "+44 1234567", "+44 2345678" ]
}

To load data, we can use the FileSystem API from Rust’s standard library. We’ll do this in our main.rs file and pass the data (aka a long string of JSON) to the parser.

// src/main.rs
use std::fs; pub mod parser; fn main() { println!("Hello, world!"); // Grab JSON file let file_path = "data/test.json".to_owned(); let contents = fs::read_to_string(file_path).expect("Couldn't find or load that file."); parser::untyped_example(&contents);
}

And we’d need to modify the parser to accept data now. You can also delete the data variable with the inline JSON since we don’t need it anymore:

// src/parser.rs
pub fn untyped_example(json_data: &str) -> Result<()> { let v: Value = serde_json::from_str(json_data)?;

Try running this with cargo run and you should get the same result as before (the “Please call…” message).

Typed data

But what if we know what the structure of our data is before we parse it? For example, we might want to parse a theme using the System UI / Styled System specification, like Chakra UI.

This would let us access our data using strictly typed structs - so instead of using v["name"] to access the name, we could get autocompletion in our IDE when we type v. and it’d finish to v.name. This is a much better developer experience, and creates more safety nets against using properties that don’t exist.

The serde_json README also provides a great example for handling typed data. We can copy paste this entirely into the [parser.rs](http://parser.rs) file.

use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
use serde_json::Result; #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct Person { name: String, age: u8, phones: Vec<String>,
} pub fn typed_example() -> Result<()> { // Some JSON input data as a &str. Maybe this comes from the user. let data = r#" { "name": "John Doe", "age": 43, "phones": [ "+44 1234567", "+44 2345678" ] }"#; // Parse the string of data into a Person object. This is exactly the // same function as the one that produced serde_json::Value above, but // now we are asking it for a Person as output. let p: Person = serde_json::from_str(data)?; // Do things just like with any other Rust data structure. println!("Please call {} at the number {}", p.name, p.phones[0]); Ok(())
}

Then replace the untyped_example function with the typed_example function in your main.rs file.

pub mod parser; fn main() { println!("Hello, world!"); // You can keep the file system stuff, I removed it for simplicity // we'll use it later in the tutorial parser::typed_example();
}

How to handle object types?

So the first question that came to mind after looking at the examples — how do you handle an object with keys and properties? It seems you can use a HashMap<> type and provide a type for the object key (usually a String) and the object value (anything — sometimes a String - maybe a i32 for numbers).

So say I had a JSON theme that looked like this:

{ "animation": { "default": "400ms ease-in", "fast": "300ms ease-in" }, "breakpoints": { "mobile": "320px", "tablet": "768px", "computer": "992px", "desktop": "1200px", "widescreen": "1920px" }, "colors": { "text": "#111212", "background": "#fff", "primary": "#005CDD", "secondary": "#6D59F0", "muted": "#f6f6f9", "gray": "#D3D7DA", "highlight": "hsla(205, 100%, 40%, 0.125)", "white": "#FFF", "black": "#111212" }, "fonts": { "body": "Roboto, Helvetiva Neue, Helvetica, Aria, sans-serif", "heading": "Archivo, Helvetiva Neue, Helvetica, Aria, sans-serif", "monospace": "Menlo, monospace" }, "font_sizes": [12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96], "font_weights": { "body": 400, "heading": 500, "bold": 700 }, "line_heights": { "body": 1.5, "heading": 1.25 }, "space": [0, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512], "sizes": { "avatar": 48 }, "radii": { "default": 0, "circle": 99999 }, "shadows": { "card": { "light": "15px 15px 35px rgba(0, 127, 255, 0.5)" } }, "gradients": { "primary": "linear-gradient()" }
}

You could structure that type to look like this:

use std::collections::HashMap; use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
use serde_json::Result; #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct Theme { colors: HashMap<String, String>, space: Vec<i32>, font_sizes: Vec<i32>, fonts: HashMap<String, String>, font_weights: HashMap<String, i32>, line_heights: HashMap<String, f32>, breakpoints: HashMap<String, String>, animation: HashMap<String, String>, gradients: HashMap<String, String>,
}

You can see I use String for any string based values, i32 for numbers, and specifically f32 for any “floats” aka numbers with decimals.

When the JSON is parsed, a HashMap is returned, so you can access the data inside using the get() method - or loop over all the values using for loop:

// Get a single value
println!("Black: {}", p.colors.get("black")); // Loop over all the colors
for (key, color) in p.colors { // Create the custom property let custom_property = format!("--colors-{}", key); let css_rule = format!("{}: {};", &custom_property, color); // @TODO: Export a CSS stylesheet file (or return CSS) println!("{}", css_rule); stylesheet.push(css_rule); // Add the custom property theme_tokens.colors.insert(key, custom_property);
}

This works pretty well! You can see here we’re able to loop over the colors and even generate CSS custom properties based on the key and value from the HashMap (aka the color name and value).

Handling optional / multiple types

But what if we have optional types? Or multiple types for the same property (like a “size” unit that could be an number 10 or a string like 10px)? In Typescript we’d be able to just create a type like this type Size = string | number. In Rust, the equivalent of this would be an Enum.

After researching a bit I found that serde supports Enum types if you pass the untagged macro to them:

#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Debug)]
#[serde(untagged)]
enum Colors { Name(String), Number(i32),
} #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct Theme { test: Colors,
} // ... after parsing
let p: Theme = serde_json::from_str(json_data)?;
println!("{:#?}", p.test);

Add the following property to your JSON:

And serde will grab from the Number(i32) Enum property and return that — you’ll need to do a match statement to figure out what it is + get value back:

match p.test { // We don't want the name so we do nothing by passing empty tuple Name(val) -> (), Number(num) -> println!("Theme Color is number: {}", num),
}

This works great too! You can easily create some “dynamic” types and still have fairly strict handling of them based on their type.

Putting the eyy back in JSON

I hope this gives you a good idea of how to parse JSON in Rust using the serde crate, and how to handle different use cases and data types. There’s lot of cool apps you can create using JSON (or other file types - serde supports lots like YAML, TOML, and more).

As always, you can find the full code for this tutorial up on my Github.

If you have any questions or want to share what you’ve been working on, feel free to hit me up on Twitter.

Cheers,
Ryo

Anurag Vishwakarma - Oct 10

item.title: The Rising Coder - 第 10/13 周（前端第 3/3 周） | The Rising Coder - Week 10/13 (Frontend Week 3/3)
item.description:
早上好，下午好，或者晚上好，又到了一周中的那个时候 - The Rising Coder 的另一个更新！

一如既往地感谢您来检查我和我在Northcoders 训练营的旅程，不再拖延，让我们直接讨论本周到底发生了什么？

第 10 周标志着 Northcoders 训练营前端部分的最后一周，男孩绝对是一段​​旅程的野兽。 从训练营开始，我们一直在努力编写一个简单的 For-Loop，并试图了解诸如map和filter之类的高阶函数数组方法，到像使用它们一样使用它们。

甚至在我记得的前端部分的开头也提到过仅仅考虑“状态”这个词在第一周就让我处于正确的状态......但是现在，我已经对它的含义有了一个坚定的理解是以及为什么我们会在 React 中使用 State。

这一切都来了一个完整的循环，我们从这个训练营的开始到结束按顺序学到的所有东西都是由 Northcoders 精心制作的，以确保我们知道在我们作为开发人员的第一个初级角色中需要掌握的一切！

够了……这周你做了什么？

由于这是我们在前端开发方面工作的最后一周，我们现在终于能够开始构建我们自己的前端网站，这些网站将使用我们在后端项目阶段创建的后端 API课程第 7/13 周，真正让这个项目成为我们自己的宝贵财产，我们将与未来的雇主分享。

甚至在进入实际的前端项目本身之前，我想尝试通过首先在我的后端 API 的根目录上创建文档来为我的项目添加一些趣味，该文档将详细说明所有可能的 API 端点和响应用户将收到，并挑战自己学习一个名为 Tailwind CSS 的新框架。

我可以说我绝对不后悔选择学习 Tailwind CSS。 对于那些对 CSS 样式没有最好理解的人来说，这是一个救命稻草。 如果您不熟悉Tailwind CSS是一个“实用程序优先的 CSS 框架”——简而言之，这意味着它带有预捆绑的 CSS 类，您可以使用它们来设置代码样式。

但是，Tailwind CSS 的美妙之处在于，您永远不必离开代码编辑器来创建单独的 CSS 文件并将它们与您的 React 组件链接起来。 这是因为所有样式都是通过将 Tailwind CSS 属性放在 JSX 元素的className中完成的，然后由您决定如何开始构建组件！

在我继续谈论我对这一周和最终前端项目的反思之前，这里是我的现场项目的链接： Christopher Lam 的 Northcoders 新闻

反思 #1 - 慢慢来

在这周我一直在完成我的项目的过程中，最重要的事情之一是我必须不断地提醒自己，以我自己的节奏慢慢来绝对没问题。 想一想你的团队中的其他人在做什么以及“他们领先多少”真的很诱人，但归根结底，他们的进步是你自己的，你的也是你的。

我承认，当我无法抗拒检查别人如何做的冲动时，我会失去信心，感觉自己“落后”，并感到压力过大时身体不适。

所以TLDR：喘口气，不要看周围的其他人和他们的进步，专注于自己！

反思 #2 - 官方文档是你最好的朋友

尽管创建 React 和其他围绕特定框架或编程语言构建的库的人知道什么是最好的，这是不言自明的，但很容易忘记这个事实！ 我自己在没有先正确查阅官方文档的情况下尝试谷歌 StackOverflow 的答案而感到内疚，这又回来咬我了（稍后会详细介绍）！

反思 #3 - 定期休息是天赐良机

对于那些能够连续几个小时坐在屏幕前而不休息的人来说，这可能适用于也可能不适用于您，但是，定期休息并在街区周围快速散步真的可以帮助你们俩获得一些维生素D，但更多。 重要的是解开你的思想！

整个星期和整个训练营的次数，我能够休息一下，然后带着那个“EUREKA！”回来。 时刻数不胜数，所以 TLDR 就是把那台笔记本电脑/电脑收起来，好好享受一下新鲜空气和维生素 D！

反思#4 - 享受自己

如果你已经在学术界工作了多年，并且你的每一项任务都必须给你带来压力并且绝对不愉快，那么这就是你在 Northcoders 训练营学到的基本上不需要学习的东西。

不要把后端和前端项目当成你的敌人，给你带来不必要的压力，而是试着从另一端考虑。

如果您真的喜欢从事您的项目，那么您对如何处理项目以及对项目的感觉的整个看法将完全改变！

例如，想象一下如果这些项目只给你带来压力，而你不得不尝试向未来的雇主解释你的项目，那么如果你只是消极地看待这个项目，就很难恰当地表达你对项目工作的兴奋。 将此与您是否真的喜欢从事该项目进行比较，以及这对未来的雇主来说会有多清晰。

所以 TLDR 真的是在尝试挑战您对后端和前端项目的看法，以便您为未来的自己提供帮助！

小惊喜

在我关闭本周这个相对较短的博客条目之前，对于那些坚持到最后的人，如果你在 LinkedIn 上与我的博客互动，请继续发表评论：你最难忘的 Northcoders 时刻经验是，为什么？

因为我对 Northcoders 体验的享受是能够结识如此令人惊叹的一群人，无论是我有幸结识的所有导师或我的同伴。

而对于最难忘的时刻，它必须是今天发生的事情......

如果你在与 Jim 的最后一次 Zoom 通话中——那么你会听到他谈论“不要使用数组的索引号作为键”。 所以，如果你还在阅读，那么是的，那就是我😂

因此，在向您展示让我们头疼的代码之前，我将解释发生了什么。 在我的一段代码中，我有一个CommentsDisplay组件，它可以映射一组comments ，在 React 中，当在 React 中映射它时，你需要将一个“键”传递给一个列表项，这样 React 就知道要做什么寻找。

然而，我是绝对的笨蛋，造成了绝对的噩梦！ 因此，当我在我的项目中实现“发表评论”和“删除评论”功能时，我能够完美地删除评论，但发表评论会暂时带回之前删除的评论。

然后我提出了一个绝望的 NC 帮助请求，斯嘉丽（一位杰出的导师）试图介入并挽救这一天……除了我们俩都倒在地上，不得不被吉姆救下……除了吉姆也完全困惑，不得不从屏幕上抽出一点时间来尝试处理我所做的绝对混乱😂

因此，事不宜迟，这是导致问题的一小段代码：

 { comments . map (({ comment_id , author , body , article_id , votes , created_at }, index //<-- THIS) => { const convertedCreationDate = created_at . slice ( 0 , 10 ) return ( < li key = { index } > //<--- AND THIS - THESE TWO LINES OF CODE < CommentCard removeComment = { removeComment } comment_id = { comment_id } author = { author } body = { body } article_id = { article_id } votes = { votes } created_at = { convertedCreationDate } / > < /li > ) })}

确实是这样，但在包括我在内的三个人目瞪口呆地盯着代码的时候，也不是那么清楚。 早些时候，当我谈到总是阅读官方文档时，我曾暗示过这一点， React的这篇文档中说：如果项目的顺序可能发生变化，我们不建议对键使用索引。 未来的想法

在第 10 周结束和训练营的前端模块中，信息量更少但更个人化的博客文章将随之而来。 接下来的三周将专注于项目阶段，开发我们的简历并为未来的技术测试做准备，我对此非常紧张，但同时也很兴奋。

但就目前而言，我只想享受我的周末，明天晚上观看英雄联盟总决赛，希望下周再次见到你们，一如既往地感谢你们的阅读！

社交

请随时关注我在下面的任何社交活动！



原文:

Good morning, good afternoon or perhaps good evening, it is that time of the week again - another update of The Rising Coder!

As always thank you for coming to check up on me and my journey at the Northcoders Bootcamp and without any further delay, let's get straight onto the topic of what exactly did happen this week?

Week 10 marks the final week of the Front-End section of the Northcoders Bootcamp, and boy has it been an absolute beast of a journey. From the start of the bootcamp where we were struggling to write a simple For-Loop and trying to get our heads around Higher Order Function Array Methods such as map and filter, to using them as if they were second nature.

Even the start of the Front-End block where I remember having mentioned that just thinking about the word "State" just left me in a right state in the first week... But now, I've got a firm understanding of what it is and why we would use State in React.

It all came full-circle and everything that we learnt in order from the start-to-end of this bootcamp have been carefully crafted by Northcoders to ensure that we know everything we need to get our hands dirty in our first Junior roles as developers!

Enough of that... What did you do this week?

As this is our final week working on the Front-End side of development, we were now able to finally start building our own Front-End websites that would consume the Back-End API that we had created during the Back-End project phase during Week 7/13 of the course, and really making this project our own prized possession that we would be sharing with future employers.

Even before going into the actual Front-End project itself, I wanted to try add a bit of spice to my projects by first creating documentation on the root directory of my Back-End API that would detail all of the possible API endpoints and responses that the user would receive, as well as challenging myself to learn a new framework called Tailwind CSS.

And I can say that I definitely do not regret my choice of learning Tailwind CSS. For someone that does not have the best understanding of CSS styling, it was a lifesaver. If you are unfamiliar with what Tailwind CSS is a "utility-first css framework" - in short this means that it comes with pre-bundled CSS classes that you just use to style your code.

But, the beautiful thing about Tailwind CSS is that you never have to leave your code editor to make separate CSS files and link them up with your React Components. This is because all of the styling is done by putting the Tailwind CSS properties in the className of the JSX elements, and then it's up to you how you start building up your components!

Before I go on to talk about my reflections of this entire week and the final Front-End project, here is a link to my live project: Christopher Lam's Northcoders News

Reflection #1 - Take It Slow

Over the course of this week where I'd been working through my project, one of the most important things I had to constantly remind myself that it's absolutely fine to take it slow and at my own pace. It's really tempting to think about what others on your cohort are doing and "how far ahead they are", but at the end of the day, their progress is your own and yours is yours.

I'd admit that I had a lapse of confidence when I couldn't resist the urge to check on how others were doing and feeling that I felt "behind" and felt that physical discomfort that came with stressing myself out.

So TLDR: take a breather, don't look at the others around you and their progress, focus on yourself!

Reflection #2 - Official Documentation's Your Best Friend

Although this is pretty self-explanatory that the people who created React and other libraries built around a certain framework or programing language know what's best, it's really easy to forget this fact! I myself am guilty of trying to Google StackOverflow answers without properly consulting the official documentation first and that's come back to bite me (more on that later)!

Reflection #3 - Regular Breaks Are A God-Send

For those of you that are able to sit at the screen for hours on end without a single break, perhaps this may or may not apply to you but, taking regular breaks and going on a quick walk around the block can really help you both get some vitamin-D, but more. importantly unblock your mind!

The number of times throughout this week and bootcamp in general, where I've been able to just take a break and then come back with that "EUREKA!" moment have been countless, so TLDR is to put that laptop/pc away and treat yourself to some fresh air and vitamin-D!

Reflection #4 - Enjoy Yourself

If you've been in academia for years and had it conditioned in you that every assignment that you're given must cause you stress and is absolutely unenjoyable, then this is what you learn to essentially un-learn here at the Northcoders Bootcamp.

Instead of fighting the Back-end and Front-End projects as if they were your enemies that just cause you unnecessary stress, instead try think of it through the other end.

If you truly enjoy working on your projects then your entire outlook on how you approach your projects and how you feel about your projects will change entirely!

For example, imagine if the projects only caused you stress and you had to try explain your projects to future employers, it'd be difficult to properly convey your excitement over working on the project at all if you only viewed the project negatively. Compare this to if you really enjoyed working on the project and how more clear-cut this would come across to future employers.

So TLDR is really try to challenge your outlook on how you perceive the Back-End and Front-End projects so that you do your future self a favour!

Mini Surprise

Before I close up this relatively shorter blog entry for this week, for those of you who have stuck around to the end, if you interact with my blogs on LinkedIn, go ahead and drop in the comments: What your most memorable moment of the Northcoders experience was, and why?

For what I enjoyed about the Northcoders Experience was being able to meet such an amazing group of people be it all of the mentors or my fellow cohort members that I've had the pleasure of meeting.

And for the most memorabe moment, it would have to be what happened today...

If you were in the final Zoom call with Jim - then you would have heard him talk about "Do not use an array's index number as a key." So if you're still reading then yes, that was me 😂

So before I show you the code that was causing us headaches, I'll just explain what happened. In my piece of code I had a CommentsDisplay component that would map over an array of comments and in React, you need to pass in a "key" into a List Item when mapping over it in React, this is so that React knows what to look for.

However, me being the absolute pillock I am, caused an absolute nightmare! So when I was implementing a "Post Comment" and "Delete Comment" feature in my project, I was able to delete the comments perfectly fine, but posting a comment would temporarily bring back previously deleted comments.

I then put in a desparate NC Help request to which Scarlett (One of the brilliant mentors) tried to step in and save the day... Except we both fell flat on the floor and had to get saved by Jim... except Jim was also completely baffled and had to take a bit of time away from the screen to try process what an absolute mess I had made 😂

So without further ado here was the small piece of code that caused the issue:

{comments.map(({ comment_id, author, body, article_id, votes, created_at }, index //<-- THIS) => {  const convertedCreationDate = created_at.slice(0, 10) return ( <li key={index} > //<--- AND THIS - THESE TWO LINES OF CODE <CommentCard removeComment={removeComment} comment_id={comment_id} author={ author } body={ body } article_id={ article_id } votes={ votes } created_at={ convertedCreationDate } />
 </li>
 ) })}

It really was just this, but it wasn't exactly so clear-cut even when three people including myself were staring at the code in complete bewilderness. Earlier on I had hinted towards this when I said something about always reading official documentation in which this article of documentation by React says: We don’t recommend using indexes for keys if the order of items may change. - And thus again highlights the fact that documentation is absolutely essential as a developer, I'm really sorry for causing a headache Scarlett and Jim, but I'm glad that I got to see a giddy side of Jim that I hadn't seen in the lectures as he was ecstatic and over the moon when he solved the problem - brilliant stuff Jim you lifesaver! 😭

Thoughts Going Forward

And with that less-informative and more personal blog post will come the end of Week 10 and the Front-End block of the bootcamp. Going forward will be the next three-weeks that are focused on the project phase and developing our CVs and preparing for future tech tests, which I am extremely nervous about but excited at the same time.

But for now, I'm just going to enjoy my weekend and watch the League of Legends Championship Finals tomorrow evening and hopefully see you all this time again next week, and as always thank you for reading!

Socials

Feel free to give me a follow on any of my socials down below!
item.author: clam119

item.title: 如何在 Android 中模拟进程死亡？ | How to Simulate Process Death in Android?
item.description:
探索模拟Android操作系统和用户发起的进程死亡的不同方式

系统启动的进程死亡

  • 用户发起的进程死亡

    • 系统发起的进程死亡意味着该进程被Android操作系统杀死。 这可能是由于资源限制（内存不足）或只是配置更改（屏幕旋转）。

    用户发起的进程死亡意味着进程被用户杀死。 例如，您单击主页按钮，然后手动关闭后台应用程序。

    通常作为开发人员，您只关心系统启动的进程死亡，因为您需要保存 UI 状态。 对于用户启动的进程死亡，您不需要保存 UI 状态。

    系统启动的进程死亡用户发起的进程死亡
    需要保存 UI 状态不需要保存 UI 状态

    对于保存 UI 状态期望，你可以参考官方文档这里。

    系统启动的进程死亡

    模拟系统启动的进程死亡的最简单方法是使用 Logcat 终止应用程序按钮。

    • 你按下主页按钮。
    • 然后，使用 Logcat 终止应用程序按钮，如下所示。

    等等，这个“终止应用程序”按钮已在 Android Studio Dolphin 中删除。 在此处查看问题跟踪器。

    根据票证，它已被移至Android Studio Flamingo版本中的设备监视器。 我刚试了，不行！ 它不能用来替换这个 Logcat Terminate App Button！

    在设备监视器中强制停止！= Logcat 终止应用程序

    在设备监视器（Android Studio Flamingo）中，有 2 个选项

    • 杀死进程 - 相当于adb shell am kill命令
    • 强制停止进程 - 相当于adb shell am force-stop命令

    杀死进程什么都不做，因为设备没有root。

    强制停止进程确实会杀死进程，但它不会模拟系统启动的进程死亡。 它模拟用户启动的进程死亡，这与使用adb shell am force-stop命令完全相同 - 见下文。

    所以，这不是我们想要的。 即使您升级到此 Android Studio Flamingo 版本，您也无法使用Device Monitor来模拟系统启动的进程死亡。

    我知道的唯一解决方法是在开发人员选项中不设置后台进程。 要启用开发者选项，请参阅此处的步骤。

    • 转到设置 -> 系统 -> 开发人员选项（取决于手机，开发人员选项可能位于不同的位置）
    • 在应用程序，后台进程限制，选择无后台进程

    模拟系统启动的进程死亡，一旦你启动了你的应用程序

    • 按主页按钮
    • 启动其他应用程序并按主页按钮

    您的应用程序进程将被杀死。 如果您使用的是 Android Studio Dolphin，您应该能够在 Logcat 中看到它。

     PROCESS ENDED (8227) for package com.example.understandlifecyclesdemo

    您还可以在 Android Studio 终端中使用adb shell ps检查进程，并确保您的应用程序包不再显示在进程列表中。

    我刚刚尝试了这种方法，它似乎不适用于 API Level 33。该过程仍在后台。 它至少在 API 级别 26 上工作（我已经成功尝试过的 API 级别）。

    用户发起的进程死亡

    模拟用户启动的进程死亡的最简单方法是完全按照用户可以执行的操作。

    按主页按钮
  • 按方形按钮
  • 关闭应用程序 - 按X或CLEAR ALL

    • 另一种方法是在终端中使用adb命令。 即使您的应用程序在前台，您也可以运行此命令。 它只会直接杀死您的应用程序。

     adb shell am force-stop <applicationId>

    请注意，这不是您的包裹名称。

     android { compileSdk 32 defaultConfig { applicationId "com.example.understandlifecyclesdemo" /*...*/ } }

    所以基于上面的applicationId名称，完整的命令看起来像这样。

     adb shell am force-stop com.example.understandlifecyclesdemo

    与上面类似，您可以使用该命令adb shell ps检查进程是否已成功杀死。

    我也尝试adb shell am kill <applicationId> 。 它对我不起作用。 什么都没有发生，没有错误。 我认为它需要您的设备植根。

    进程死亡恢复

    在玩保存 UI 状态时，我注意到对于用户启动的进程死亡恢复，即使您在应用程序被终止之前将数据保存到包中，Android 操作系统也不会恢复 UI 状态。 具体来说，没有调用Activity.onRestoreInstanceState() 。 在Activity.onCreate()中， Bundle参数为空。

    对于系统启动的进程死亡恢复，如果在进程死亡之前保存了数据，Android OS 会恢复 UI 状态。 具体来说，调用Activity.onRestoreInstanceState() 。 在Activity.onCreate()中， Bundle参数不为空。

    这类似于视图模型中的SavedStateHandle 。 对于用户启动的进程死亡恢复，即使您在此之前保存了数据，从SavedStateHandle检索到的值也是 null。 对于系统启动的进程死亡恢复，检索到的值是之前保存的值。

    这是摘要

    系统启动的进程死亡恢复用户启动的进程死亡恢复
    不调用onRestoreInstanceState()
    视图模型中的SavedStateHandle与 Activity 中的Bundle是分开的。 从Bundle中保存的任何内容都无法从SavedStateHandle中检索。

    结论

    如果您在 Android Studio Dolphin 或更高版本上，模拟系统启动进程死亡的唯一方法是在开发人员选项中设置无后台进程，但不知何故，它在 API 级别 33 上不起作用。所以解决方法是使用 API 级别26. 它可能适用于其他 API 级别。

    源代码

    最初发布于https://vtsen.hashnode.dev 。



    原文:

    Explore Different Ways of Simulating Process Death Initiated by the Android Operating System and the User

    There are 2 types of process death in Android:

    • System-initiated Process Death
    • User-initiated Process Death

    System-initiated process death means the process is killed by the Android operating system. It could be due to resource constraints (not enough memory) or simply configuration changes (screen rotation).

    User-initiated process death means the process is killed by the user. For example, you click the home button, and manually close the background app.

    Usually as a developer, you care about only the system-initiated process death because you're required to save the UI state. For user-initiated process death, you do NOT need to save the UI states.

    System-initiated Process Death User-initiated Process Death
    Required to save UI states NOT required to save UI states

    For saving UI states expectation, you can refer to the official documentation here.

    System-initiated Process Death

    1. Use Logcat Terminate App Button

    The easiest way to simulate system-initiated process death, use the Logcat terminate app button.

    • You press the home button.
    • Then, use the Logcat terminate app button as shown below.

    Wait, this "Terminate App" button has been REMOVED in Android Studio Dolphin. See the issue tracker here.

    According to the ticket, it has been moved to Device Monitor in Android Studio Flamingo version. I just tried, it does NOT work! It can NOT be used to replace this Logcat Terminate App Button!

    Force Stop in Device Monitor != Logcat Terminate App

    In device monitor (Android Studio Flamingo), there are 2 options

    • Kill process - equivalent to adb shell am kill command
    • Force stop process - equivalent to adb shell am force-stop command

    Kill process does nothing because the device is not rooted.

    Force stop process does kill the process, BUT it does NOT simulate system-initiated process death. It simulates user-initiated process death, which is exactly the same as using adb shell am force-stop command - see below.

    So, this is not what we want. Even if you upgrade to this Android Studio Flamingo version, you won't able to use Device Monitor to simulate system-initiated process death.

    2. Set No Background Processes - Developer Options

    The only workaround that I am aware is set no background processes in developer options. To enable developer options, you refer to the step here.

    • Go to Setting -> System -> Developer Options (depends on Phone, Developer Options could be at different location)
    • In Apps, Background process limit, select No background processes

    To simulate system-initiated process death, once you have launched your app

    • Press the Home button
    • Launch other app and press the Home button

    Your app process will be killed. You should be able to see that in your Logcat if you're using Android Studio Dolphin.

    PROCESS ENDED (8227) for package com.example.understandlifecyclesdemo

    You can also inspect the process using the adb shell ps in Android Studio terminal and make sure your app package is no longer shown up in the process list.

    I just tried this method and it doesn't seem to work on API Level 33. The process is still at the background. It worked on API level 26 at least (the API level that I've tried sucessfully).

    User-initiated Process Death

    The easiest way to simulate user-initiated process death is doing exactly what user can do.

    1. Manually Close the App

    • Press the Home button
    • Press the Square button
    • Close the App - press the X or CLEAR ALL

    2. adb shell am force-stop

    Another way is using the adb command in the terminal. You can run this command even your app is in foreground. It will just kill your app directly.

    adb shell am force-stop <applicationId>

    The applicationId is the one you define in the build.gradle app level. Please note, it is NOT your package name.

    android { compileSdk 32 defaultConfig { applicationId "com.example.understandlifecyclesdemo" /*...*/ }
}

    So the full command looks like this based on the above applicationId name.

    adb shell am force-stop com.example.understandlifecyclesdemo

    Similar to above, you can check whether the process has been successfully killed using this command adb shell ps.

    I also try adb shell am kill <applicationId>. It doesn't work for me. Nothing happens, no errors. I think it requires your device to be rooted.

    Process Death Recovery

    While playing with saving UI states, I notice for user-initiated process death recovery, Android OS does NOT restore the UI states even you save data into the bundle before your app is killed. Specifically, Activity.onRestoreInstanceState() is not called. In Activity.onCreate(), the Bundle parameter is null.

    For system-initiated process death recovery, Android OS restores the UI states if there are data being saved prior to process death. Specifically, Activity.onRestoreInstanceState() is called. In Activity.onCreate(), the Bundle parameter is NOT null.

    This is similar to SavedStateHandle in your view model. For user-initiated process death recovery, retrieved value from SavedStateHandle is null even you save the data prior to that. For system-initiated process death recovery, the retrieved value is the previously saved value.

    Here is the summary

    System-initiated Process Death Recovery User-initiated Process Death Recovery
    onRestoreInstanceState() is called onRestoreInstanceState()is NOT called
    Bundle is NOT null in onCreate() Bundle is null in onCreate()
    SavedStateHandle.get() returns previously saved value SavedStateHandle.get() returns null

    SavedStateHandle in view model is seperated from the Bundle in the activity. Whatever being saved from the Bundle can NOT be retrieved from SavedStateHandle.

    Conclusion

    If you're on Android Studio Dolphin or later, the only way to simulate system-initiated process death is Set No Background Processes in Developer Options, but somehow it doesn't work on API level 33. So the workaround is to use API level 26. It may work on other API levels.

    Source Code

    GitHub Repository: Demo_UnderstandLifecycles

    Originally published at https://vtsen.hashnode.dev.

一旦取消发布，official_dreamz 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 official_dreamz 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 您可以执行以下操作来标记 official_dreamz：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记 official_dreamz 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Once suspended, official_dreamz will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, official_dreamz will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by official_dreamz will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If official_dreamz is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Thanks for keeping DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻 safe. Here is what you can do to flag official_dreamz:

official_dreamz consistently posts content that violates DEV Community 👩‍💻👨‍💻's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging official_dreamz will restore default visibility to their posts.

item.title: 四天后熨斗学校 | Flatiron School, Four Days Later
item.description:
内森·乌尔诺斯基

发表于我的名字是内森。 我住在马里兰州南部的郊区，离安纳波利斯、哥伦比亚特区和巴尔的摩（按此顺序）只有几个小时的路程。 小时候，我一直很喜欢技术。 从在 Windows XP 上玩 Pajama Sam 和 Space Cadet Pinball 的日子，到在我活着的 22 年里发布的几乎所有现代主流视频游戏机的试验。 那是一口……我什至记得这些年来我是我的几位老师的非官方 IT 人员。

在高中时，我参加了 Cisco Networking Academy 课程，开始了我的技术之旅。 我什至能够获得入门级网络的 CCENT 认证（现在称为 CCNA 认证）。 当然，就在大流行使世界天翻地覆之前，我高中毕业了。 在那段时间里，我还处理了我祖母和兄弟的去世，加上两所大学的起步缓慢和一份短暂而糟糕的便利店工作，我处于低谷。

在去年初开始了一份新的 IT 工作后，我向各种技术途径敞开了大门。 这就是我参加编码训练营的原因，纽约的熨斗学校引起了我的特别注意。 这就是我今天写作的原因，因为这是毕业要求。

当然，在坦率地写这篇文章时，我是一个非常有文字的巫师。 如果您选择加入我的旅程，请注意我在写作中确实会漫无边际。

不幸的是，我今天没有可分享的代码。 我只是想在今天早些时候在百思买买的这款新 MacBook 上打字时分享我的想法。 还不错的键盘。

感谢您的阅读。 我希望将来在我的帖子中更有用。

暂停后，birdsthe3rdword 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被取消。

一旦解除暂停，birdsthe3rdword 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦未发布，birdsthe3rdword 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果birdsthe3rdword 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

感谢您保护 DEV 社区👩‍💻👨‍💻 的安全。 您可以执行以下操作来标记birdsthe3rdword：

举报其他不当行为

取消标记birdsthe3rdword 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Nathan Urnosky

Posted on Nov 4

My name is Nathan. I live in a suburb of Southern Maryland, a few hours away from Annapolis, DC, and Baltimore (in that order). As a kid, I was always into technology. From the days of playing Pajama Sam and Space Cadet Pinball on Windows XP, to experimenting with pretty much every modern mainstream video game console that's been released in the 22 years I've been alive. That was a mouthful... I even remember being the unofficial IT guy for several of my teachers over the years.

In High School, I began my technology journey by taking part in a Cisco Networking Academy class. I was even able to obtain the CCENT certification for entry-level networking (now known as the CCNA certification). Of course, I graduated High School right before the pandemic turned the world upside down. Having also dealt with the death of both my Grandma and Brother in that time, plus a slow start at two colleges and a short, crappy convenience store job, I was at a low point.

After starting a new IT job at the beginning of last year, I've opened myself up to all sorts of technology avenues. That is what lead me to coding bootcamps, with the New York-based Flatiron School catching my particular attention. This is why I write today, as it's a graduation requirement.

Of course, I am quite a wizard with words when it comes to writing this candidly. If you choose to join me on my journey, be aware that I do ramble and run-on in my writing quite a bit.

I unfortunately have no code to share today. I just wanted to share my thoughts while typing on this new MacBook I bought at Best Buy earlier today. Damn good keyboard, too.

Thank you for reading. I hope to be a bit more useful in my posts in the future.

item.title: A Difícil Escolha de Carreira do Desenvolvedor | A Difícil Escolha de Carreira do Desenvolvedor
item.description:
伊尼西奥

观察：não recomendo esse tipo de aprendizado para ninguém！

埃斯塔吉奥

初级

决定与 TL;DR

Bom, tomar decisões sobre alteração de stack, de frente, etc, foi sempre algo natural para mim, depois de entender bem a lógica e migrar bastante de linguagens você começa a entender que tudo segue o mesmo padrão, por isso na faculdade existe aulas de Lógica de Programação. Bom, tomar decisões sobre alteração de stack, de frente, etc, foi semper algo natural para mim, depois de entender bem a lógica e migrar bastante de linguagens você começa a entender que tudo segue o mesmo padrão, por isso na faculdade existse aulas de程序逻辑。

Para você que pode estar passando por dificuldades do que escolher, segue esses passos simples que pode te ajudar a decidir: Para você que pode estar passando por dificuldades do que escolher, segue esses passos simples que pode te ajudar a decidir:

1 - Escolha uma linguagem que você já tem familiaridade para começar; 1 - Escolha uma linguagem que você já tem familiaridade para começar; 2 - Tente estudar a frente oposta a ela (Ex.: Estou gostando de Python para backend vou estudar mais de HTML, CSS, JavaScript para front) 3 - Tente começar com as vagas disponíveis no mercado utilizando a sua stack já escolhida, seja ela FullStack ou somente uma frente. 2 - Tente estudar a frente oposta a ela（例如：Estou gostando de Python para backend vou estudar mais de HTML, CSS, JavaScript para front） 3 - Tente começar com as vagas disponíveis no mercado utilizando a sua stack já escolhida, seja ela FullStack ou somete uma frente。 4 - Siga sempre aprendendo, não é ruim trocar sua frente ou as linguagens que utiliza, na verdade, isso te da mais versatilidade em um futuro. 4 - Siga semper aprendendo, não é ruim trocar sua frente ou as linguagens que utiliza, na verdade, isso te da mais Versatilidade em um futuro。

5 - Tenha consciência que você está feliz com o que está trabalhando, se não estiver, tente trocar uma ideia com o seu gestor, às vezes ele te conhece melhor e pode te orientar, ele provavelmente já passou por isso. 5 - Tenha consciência que você está feliz com o que está trabalhando, se não estiver,tente trocar uma ideia com o seu gestor, às vezes ele te conhece melhor e pode te orientar, ele provavelmente já passou por isso。



原文:

Nesse post, vou falar um pouco da minha história, minhas escolhas e tentar ajudar a direcionar melhor quem está em dúvida sobre como seguir a carreira de desenvolvedor.

Meu Início

Comecei minha carreira aos 16 anos, mas já estava programando desde os 14 com PHP padrão, sem nenhum framework. Nunca escolhi o que estaria utilizando, pois veio tudo durante meu aprendizado.

Sou um Dev autodidata, então naquela época eu não sabia pesquisar, eu simplesmente procurava como fazer um sistema de login usando PHP e MySQL, porque eu tinha visto em um tutorial anterior.
Obs.: não recomendo esse tipo de aprendizado para ninguém!

Estágio

Após os meus 16, onde comecei com meu primeiro estágio, comecei a entender que eu era FullStack, e a partir dai segui convicto sobre o que eu queria seguir, queria seguir a carreira FullStack, pois faço tudo e gosto de fazer tudo.

Júnior

Depois de um tempo que saí do estágio, entrei como Júnior FullStack utilizando NodeJS em uma startup, simplesmente maravilhado com a ideia de poder fazer desde a lógica dos dados, até o que o cliente vai ver no final, me sentia um Deus.

Passaram-se uns meses e minha percepção sobre o FullStack mudou, não gostava mais daquele mundo, me sentia sobrecarregado exercendo muitas tarefas e comecei a realmente gostar do Backend, falei com o meu gestor da época, que foi extremamente solicito, e foquei meu desenvolvimento em Backend, simples assim.

Pleno, Head, etc

Hoje sigo na carreira de Backend, focando sempre em melhorar minhas skills, mas se surgir uma vaga de FullStack provavelmente irei considerar, pois mesmo sendo Backend o que eu realmente gosto é de programar, liderar, arquitetar, e pensar, e tenho em mente que esse será sempre meu diferencial.

Decisões & TL;DR

Bom, tomar decisões sobre alteração de stack, de frente, etc, foi sempre algo natural para mim, depois de entender bem a lógica e migrar bastante de linguagens você começa a entender que tudo segue o mesmo padrão, por isso na faculdade existe aulas de Lógica de Programação.

Para você que pode estar passando por dificuldades do que escolher, segue esses passos simples que pode te ajudar a decidir:

1 - Escolha uma linguagem que você já tem familiaridade para começar; 2 - Tente estudar a frente oposta a ela (Ex.: Estou gostando de Python para backend vou estudar mais de HTML, CSS, JavaScript para front) 3 - Tente começar com as vagas disponíveis no mercado utilizando a sua stack já escolhida, seja ela FullStack ou somente uma frente. 4 - Siga sempre aprendendo, não é ruim trocar sua frente ou as linguagens que utiliza, na verdade, isso te da mais versatilidade em um futuro.

5 - Tenha consciência que você está feliz com o que está trabalhando, se não estiver, tente trocar uma ideia com o seu gestor, às vezes ele te conhece melhor e pode te orientar, ele provavelmente já passou por isso.
